title: Guilt Words guilt culpability remorse

pages.text:
223
Emotion Regulation Handout 6   (p. 10 of 10)
Guilt Words
guilt
culpability
remorse
apologetic
regret
sorry
Prompting Events for Feeling Guilt
•• Doing or thinking something you believe is
wrong.
•• Doing or thinking something that violates your
personal values.
•• Not doing something you said that you would
do.
•• Committing a transgression against another
person or something you value.
•• Causing harm/damage to another person or
object.
•• Causing harm/damage to yourself.
•• Being reminded of something wrong you did
in the past.
•• Other:

Interpretations of Events That Prompt Feelings of Guilt
•• Thinking that your actions are to blame for
something.
•• Thinking that you behaved badly.
•• Thinking, “If only I had done something
differently . . . ”
•• Other:
Biological Changes and Experiences of Guilt
•• Hot, red face.
•• Jitteriness, nervousness.
•• Suffocating.
•• Other:
Expressions and Actions of Guilt
•• Trying to repair the harm, make amends for the wrongdoing, fix the damage, change the
outcome.
•• Asking for forgiveness, apologizing, confessing.
•• Giving gifts/making sacrifices to try to make up for the transgression.
•• Bowing your head; kneeling before the person.
Aftereffects of Guilt
•• Making resolutions to change.
•• Making changes in behavior.
•• Joining self-help programs.
•• Other:
Other Important Emotion Words
•• Weariness, dissatisfaction, disinclination.
•• Distress.
•• Shyness, fragility, reserve, bashfulness, coyness, reticence.
•• Cautiousness, reluctance, suspiciousness, caginess, wariness.
•• Surprise, amazement, astonishment, awe, startle, wonder.
•• Boldness, bravery, courage, determination.
•• Powerfulness, a sense of competence, capability, mastery.
•• Dubiousness, skepticism, doubtfulness.
•• Apathy, boredom, dullness, ennui, fidgetiness, impatience, indifference, listlessness.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Guilt Words guilt culpability remorse
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.
